resume:
Priya Nandakumar
PhD Candidate, Computational Neuroscience (Year 4) | Applied ML Research Intern Candidate
priya.nandakumar@university.edu | Cambridge, MA

SUMMARY
Fourth-year doctoral researcher studying how recurrent neural circuits encode short-term memory, with a parallel applied-ML track in representation learning. My dissertation combines large-scale electrophysiology recordings with sequence models to test theories of working-memory maintenance. I am seeking a summer research-science internship where I can contribute to representation-learning or interpretability research and ship reproducible experiments. I care deeply about clean experimental design, honest baselines, and reproducibility, and I enjoy the collaborative grind of a research group where ideas get torn apart and rebuilt in lab meeting.

RESEARCH EXPERIENCE
Graduate Researcher, Sensory Dynamics Lab (2021-present)
My lab studies the dynamics of cortical population activity during delayed-response tasks. I joined as the first student on a new two-photon imaging rig and have grown the project into the lab's main working-memory effort. Our group of six — two postdocs, three PhD students, and a research engineer — meets weekly to critique each other's analyses, and a lot of what I know about rigorous science I learned by being wrong in those meetings and getting corrected. Over the past three years our team built a shared analysis pipeline in Python that ingests raw imaging frames, performs motion correction, segments cells, and aligns neural activity to behavioral events. We standardized on this pipeline so that every project in the lab produces comparable figures, and it has become the backbone of three papers. On the modeling side, our collaboration with a nearby ML group produced a family of recurrent and transformer sequence models that we fit to the neural data to ask which architectures best reproduce the observed memory dynamics. We presented this work at two conferences and the preprint is under review at a systems-neuroscience journal. I also helped onboard two rotation students into our codebase, walking them through the pipeline and our data-versioning conventions so they could be productive quickly.

Applied ML Research Assistant, University AI Initiative (2022-2023, part-time)
This was a cross-departmental effort to apply self-supervised representation learning to scientific time-series. Our team of four explored contrastive and masked-prediction objectives on multivariate sensor data, and we benchmarked learned representations on a battery of downstream probing tasks. We wrote a small internal library so that everyone on the team could swap encoders and objectives without rewriting boilerplate, and we maintained a shared leaderboard so results stayed honest and comparable. The initiative produced a workshop paper and an internal technical report that other groups on campus reused. I contributed to the experimental harness and to the writeup, and I learned a lot about how a well-run research team keeps experiments reproducible at scale.

Undergraduate Thesis, Vision & Learning Group (2019-2020)
For my undergraduate thesis our small group investigated robustness of image classifiers under distribution shift. We curated evaluation sets, ran controlled corruption studies, and wrote up our findings. The professor and a senior PhD student guided the framing, and we met often to align on methodology. This was my first taste of how a research group operates and it convinced me to pursue a doctorate.

TEACHING AND SERVICE
Graduate Teaching Assistant, Machine Learning for Scientists (2022, 2023)
Our teaching team of one instructor and three TAs designed problem sets and ran lab sections for roughly ninety graduate students from across the sciences. We held weekly staff meetings to calibrate grading and to redesign assignments that students found confusing. I led two lab sections and contributed to the autograder that the whole teaching team relied on.

Reviewer and Reading Group
I co-organize our lab's weekly reading group, where we rotate who presents and collectively dissect a recent paper. This is where our team forms its shared taste in what counts as convincing evidence, and it shaped how I think about baselines and ablations.

SKILLS
Python, PyTorch, JAX, NumPy, scientific computing, experiment tracking, signal processing, large-scale electrophysiology and imaging analysis, reproducible research workflows, scientific writing, LaTeX, version control, slurm-based cluster computing.

SELECTED OUTPUT
Two conference presentations on neural sequence modeling of working memory; one workshop paper on self-supervised representation learning for scientific time-series; one journal preprint under review; one internal technical report reused across campus groups. All of these were collaborative efforts produced by the teams described above.

SELECTED PROJECTS IN DETAIL
Working-memory sequence modeling: Our team set out to ask whether the geometry of cortical population activity during a delay period is better captured by a continuous-attractor recurrent model or by an attention-based sequence model. We fit several model families to the same recordings, held out trials in a principled way, and compared how well each model predicted single-trial neural trajectories. We ran ablations stripping out recurrence, varying context length, and shuffling the temporal order of inputs to confirm the models were actually using memory rather than picking up on stimulus correlations. We tracked every run in a shared experiment log so any team member could reproduce a figure from a config file. The whole effort was coordinated in weekly meetings where we argued about which baselines were fair and which ablations were load-bearing.

Self-supervised time-series representations: For the AI Initiative our group compared contrastive objectives against masked-reconstruction objectives on multivariate sensor streams. We standardized the probing protocol so that every encoder was evaluated on the same frozen-feature downstream tasks, and we maintained a leaderboard that the whole team trusted. We learned that naive contrastive augmentations destroyed exactly the temporal structure the downstream tasks needed, which reshaped how the team designed augmentations for the rest of the project.

Reproducibility practices I helped the team adopt: configuration-as-code for every experiment, deterministic seeds logged with results, data versioning so figures could be traced back to a commit, and a code-review norm where no analysis went into a paper without a second team member rerunning it. These were group decisions that I helped push for and helped implement in our shared codebase.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
A summer where I can plug into an established research team, take ownership of a focused question, and contribute reproducible experiments end to end while learning from people who are sharper than me.
jd: Research Science Intern (Representation Learning), Summer. Our applied research team investigates self-supervised and interpretability methods for foundation models, and we are hiring a PhD intern for a focused twelve-week project. You will own one research question end to end: form a hypothesis, design controlled experiments, build the training and evaluation harness, run ablations on our cluster, and write up results the team can reproduce. We expect interns to drive their own project — your mentor will unblock and critique, but the experimental design, the code, and the analysis are yours to lead. Strong candidates have first-author or core-contributor research output, fluency in PyTorch or JAX, comfort with large-scale training, and the ability to articulate exactly which parts of a project they personally designed and implemented. Deliverables: a reproducible codebase, an internal tech report, and a talk to the team. We value honest baselines and rigorous ablations over flashy results.

history.q: Tell me about the working-memory modeling project in your lab. What was the central question you were trying to answer?
Your resume mentions a family of recurrent and transformer models fit to neural data. Which specific architecture choices did you make, and why?
When you say the team built the analysis pipeline — I want to understand your slice. What part of that pipeline did you personally design and write?
Walk me through one experiment on that project where you, individually, made the key methodological call. What was the call and what happened?

candidate_last_answer: So the way our lab works is really collaborative — we sit down in lab meeting and we hash out the experimental design together, and then we divide up the analysis. For the memory-dynamics experiments, we decided as a group which models to fit and we agreed on the evaluation, and then we ran everything through our shared pipeline. We got the ablations done and we wrote it up together. Honestly the strength of the project was that it was a real team effort — nobody was off doing their own thing.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: we decided as a group which models to fit

expected-yield: the one architecture or evaluation decision the candidate personally proposed and defended in lab meeting, including what they argued for, who pushed back, and what they implemented themselves versus what a labmate built
